narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and personal circumstances to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 25, Year 1 school decile of 5, Year 3 decile of 3, and plans to attend full-time—and predicted a 71% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high predicted failure risk is why your application was rejected, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain their standards. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your LSAT score, which is well below the typical student's average of around 37 and signaled to the model a lower likelihood of bar success compared to higher-scoring peers. Next in importance, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the average of about 5.5—further reinforced concerns, as it reflects performance in the upper years of school that strongly correlates with bar outcomes for students like you. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 5, close to the average of 5.7, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, showing early promise that helped balance things out a bit. Overall, your profile stands out as riskier than typical admitted students, whose stronger LSATs and consistent decile rankings predict bar passage rates above 70%. While your full-time status aligns with most students (over 90%), it didn't outweigh the key academic red flags here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances overcome these hurdles—but the model weighs these metrics heavily based on historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly above average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or prep, and reapplying to schools with holistic reviews that might value your unique story. Building a stronger academic narrative could shift future predictions in your favor, opening doors to programs eager for diverse talents like yours. With targeted effort, you're positioning yourself for success beyond this setback.
